mnemonics.title: Power Equation Components
mnemonics.content: P = αCV²f - Remember as 'All Cats Very Fast' (Alpha, C, Voltage squared, frequency)

mnemonics.title: Energy Optimization Priority
mnemonics.content: Memory > Cache > Register - Energy cost hierarchy for data access operations

acronyms.usage: Compiler technique to adjust processor power states based on code characteristics
Metric balancing energy consumption and performance delay
Technique to execute multiple instructions simultaneously
Hardware unit whose activity affects switching factor (α)
Energy optimization that reduces post-execution idle power

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a square (V²) with height f, filled with switching activity (α). The area represents power. Compilers can 'flatten' α by balancing 0/1 transitions.
Think of a pyramid: Disk at top (most energy), then RAM, then Cache, then Registers (least energy). Optimizations try to keep data at the lowest level.
A thermometer with 'hot' code at the top (frequently executed) and 'cold' at bottom. Apply different optimization strategies at each level.

analogies.analogy: Like reading a book: instead of turning pages for each sentence (loop), read entire paragraphs at once (unrolled). Fewer page turns = less effort.
Like keeping frequently used items on your desk (registers) instead of walking to the shelf (memory) each time. Desk is energy-efficient!
Like removing unnecessary light switches that control nothing. You save the energy of flipping a switch that has no effect.
Like organizing assembly line workers to minimize waiting time. When one station waits, energy is wasted.
